fund.name: Nippon India Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Your core engine — a dirt-cheap (0.20% yearly fee) index fund that owns India's 150 mid-sized companies. Mid-caps grow faster than large-caps over long periods, and buying the whole index means no fund-manager gamble. At 40%, this is the backbone of the portfolio.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Smallcap 250 Index Fund- Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Your small-cap satellite — the growth kicker you asked for. This passive fund tracks 250 small companies at just 0.20% yearly fee. Small-caps are volatile but over 20 years they tend to deliver the highest returns. This is the risk-on portion of the plan.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Geographic diversification — you own America's 500 biggest companies (Apple, Microsoft, Google…) at 0.20% fee. Indian and US markets don't always move together, so this reduces the risk of everything crashing at once. Crucial for an NRI-like profile spending months at sea.

Gold is your insurance policy — it tends to rise when stocks fall. 10% is enough to cushion the blow during market crashes without dragging down overall returns. Note: gold funds are taxed at your income slab (no LTCG benefit), but the diversification benefit outweighs this.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Overnight Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Your parking lot — when you come ashore and have a lump sum to invest, park it here first, then move it to the equity index funds in 2-3 tranches over a few weeks. This avoids the mistake of investing everything on a single day when the market might be high.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Kuvera, Groww, or Zerodha Coin. Choose 'Direct' plans — they save you 0.5-1% every year compared to regular plans.

Step 2: Deploy your existing ₹20 lakh as a lump sum — but NOT all on day one. Put ₹1 lakh in the Overnight Fund. Then move ₹3 lakh each week from the Overnight Fund into the 4 other funds in the ratios shown (40/25/20/10). This takes ~7 weeks and protects you from investing everything on a bad day.

Step 3: Set up 5 monthly SIPs totalling ₹60,000 — they'll run automatically even when you're at sea for 8 months.

Step 4: When you come ashore and have savings to invest as a lump sum, park it in the Overnight Fund first, then move it into the 4 growth funds in 2-3 weekly tranches.

Step 5: Opt for 'Growth' option (not IDCW) for all funds — profits stay invested and compound faster.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check that all 5 SIPs are running without failed transactions. Ensure your bank account has sufficient balance before sailing — set up an auto-debit mandate.
After 1 year: Compare each fund's return to its category average. Don't panic if small-caps are down — that's normal. Only worry if a fund consistently underperforms its own index for 2+ years.

Every year on the same date: Rebalance if any fund's allocation has drifted more than 5% from target. For example, if small-caps have grown to 32% of portfolio (vs 25% target), sell the extra and buy whichever fund is below target.
Every 5 years: Reduce equity by 5% and move to gold + overnight. By age 55, you should be ~65% equity / 20% gold / 15% overnight, reducing risk as retirement approaches.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, on a fixed date (say, January 15th), check your allocation. If any fund is more than 5% away from its target, sell some of the overweight fund and buy the underweight one. Example: if mid-cap index has grown to 47% (target 40%), sell 7% and distribute to funds below target. Never rebalance more than once a year — frequent tinkering creates tax events and rarely helps. As you cross age 50, start gradually shifting 5% per year from equity to gold and overnight funds so that by retirement you're not 85% in stocks.
plan.rationale: You're 40 with 20 years until retirement, no liabilities, and an aggressive mindset — this plan matches that. The portfolio is 85% stocks (mid-cap + small-cap + US), 10% gold, 5% cash. Over 20 years at ~12.3% expected returns, your ₹20 lakh lump sum + ₹60k/month SIP reaches roughly ₹8 Cr — right on target for your retirement corpus goal. The mid-cap index is the steady engine, small-cap is the turbo charger, S&P 500 is the foreign diversification that protects you when Indian markets struggle, and gold is the shock absorber. Since you're in the new tax regime, ELSS offers no extra benefit — skip it entirely. All equity funds here are equity-taxed (LTCG 12.5% on gains above ₹1.25L/year), which is the most tax-efficient treatment available. The small-cap satellite you requested is 25% — aggressive enough for growth, not so much that a bad decade ruins the plan. The 5% overnight fund is your tactical cash reserve for smart lump-sum deployments when you return from sea.
risks: Small-cap stocks can fall 40-50% in a bad year. Your portfolio value will temporarily drop significantly. This is the price of higher long-term returns — you must stay invested and not sell in panic.
Mid-cap stocks can also drop 30-35% in a crash. Both your core (40%) and satellite (25%) are in smaller companies, so a severe downturn will hurt more than a large-cap-heavy portfolio.
The S&P 500 fund carries currency risk — if the rupee strengthens against the dollar, your US returns shrink in rupee terms. This is a risk you want: it diversifies away India-specific problems.
Gold has no yield — it only goes up in price. In long bull markets for stocks, gold can lag for years. Its job is crisis protection, not growth.
All projections are based on historical averages. Actual returns will vary year to year and could be lower than expected, especially in the first few years if markets are elevated.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year — your merchant navy salary likely grows with seniority. This single step takes your ₹8 Cr target from 'barely achievable' to 'comfortably exceeded'. At 10% step-up, you'd hit roughly ₹12+ Cr by age 60.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Starting allocation — aggressive, 20 years to retirement
Age 50 — 10 years to retirement, reduce volatility
Age 55 — 5 years out, protect what you've built
Age 60 — retirement. Enough growth, preserve capital

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction: The equity allocation (85% of total portfolio) is entirely in index funds (Nifty Midcap 150, Nifty Smallcap 250, S&P 500 Index), all explicitly described as passive with low expense ratios. No active equity funds are included. Thus, 100% of equity is passive.

turnover_score: Rebalancing is recommended only once per year, and only if allocations deviate by more than 5%. Tactical shifts are minimal, with gradual de-risking over decades. The plan explicitly warns against frequent tinkering. This reflects a strong buy-and-hold orientation.

cost_emphasis_score: The plan emphasizes 'dirt-cheap' fees (0.20% expense ratio), recommends direct plans to save 0.5-1%, and avoids ELSS due to tax regime — showing cost minimization is a primary criterion.

research_vs_cost_score: No individual stock or fund manager analysis is mentioned. Fund selection is based on index exposure and low cost, not on management quality, moats, or company fundamentals. Entirely relies on broad market exposure.

time_horizon_alignment_score: The strategy is built for a 20-year horizon, with projections to age 60 and rebalancing every 5 years to gradually reduce risk. SIP step-up aligns with long-term income growth. Entirely consistent with long-term compounding.

portfolio_activeness_score: All equity funds are index funds with explicit passive categorization. Although risk metrics (beta, tracking error) are missing, the category and rationale confirm passive intent. Gold ETF and overnight fund are standard passive instruments. No active management is present in any sleeve. The portfolio is genuinely passive across asset classes.

pqs.reasoning: The investment plan is highly aligned with Captain Haddock's aggressive risk appetite, 20-year horizon, and financial goals (especially building an 8Cr corpus). The 85% equity allocation (mid-cap, small-cap, US index) is justified and targets long-term growth, while gold and overnight funds serve tactical roles. Diversification is strong across market caps (mid, small), geographies (India, US), and asset classes (equity, gold, cash), though it lacks exposure to Indian large-caps or debt for income, which is acceptable given the investor's preference for lump-sum investing and growth focus. Risk-return is appropriate: the plan acknowledges high volatility in small/mid-caps and provides clear risk disclosures, while projected returns (~12.3%) are realistic for the allocation. The plan is internally consistent: rationale, allocations, rebalancing, and de-risking schedule all coherently support the long-term aggressive strategy. Tax efficiency is excellent: the investor is in the 'new_regime' (no 80C benefit), so ELSS is correctly omitted. All equity and index funds are equity-taxed (LTCG 12.5% >1yr), which is optimal. The 5% overnight fund is used tactically for lump-sum deployment, not as a yield-generating asset, so slab-taxation is acceptable. Gold is correctly flagged as slab-taxed, and the rationale acknowledges the tax cost but justifies it for diversification. No tax-inefficient debt funds are used for yield, avoiding unnecessary tax drag. The plan explicitly addresses tax treatment, making it tax-aware and optimal for the investor's regime.
